anionGap.title: Anion Gap Calculator
anionGap.description: Calculate anion gap from sodium, chloride, and bicarbonate with optional albumin correction and clinical interpretation.
anionGap.albuminHint: Enter only if you want the albumin-corrected value (normal: 4.0 g/dL).
anionGap.warningPlausibility: Warning: Input value is outside the physiologic range. Please check your input.

hint.low: Causes: hypoalbuminemia, bromide, lithium toxicity, hyperchloremia.
hint.normal: Anion gap within normal range (8–12 mEq/L).
hint.high: Suspect high-AG metabolic acidosis. Consider MUDPILES: Methanol, Uremia, DKA, Propylene glycol, Iron/Isoniazid, Lactic acidosis, Ethylene glycol, Salicylates.

anionGap.blogLink: Read more in the anion gap article

faq.a: The anion gap (AG) is a calculated parameter from serum electrolytes: AG = Na⁺ − (Cl⁻ + HCO₃⁻). It helps narrow the cause of metabolic acidosis.
Albumin contributes to negative charge in serum. In hypoalbuminemia the AG is falsely low. The formula AG + 2.5 × (4.0 − albumin) corrects for this effect.

faq.q: What are the most common causes of a high anion gap?
faq.a: MUDPILES: Methanol, Uremia, Diabetic Ketoacidosis (DKA), Propylene glycol, Iron/Isoniazid, Lactic acidosis, Ethylene glycol, Salicylates.

faq.q: What causes a low anion gap?
faq.a: An AG < 8 mEq/L is most often caused by hypoalbuminemia, hyperchloremia, bromide intoxication, or lithium therapy. Measurement error is also possible.

faq.q: Does this calculator replace medical diagnosis?
faq.a: No. The anion gap is a tool for clinical differential diagnosis and must always be interpreted in the context of history, symptoms, and other laboratory values.
